tier_patterns.name: tier1_task_only_pattern
tier_patterns.description: Minimal pattern for creative / exploratory agents where only the Task component is defined.

task_fields.name: objective
task_fields.description: One-sentence statement of what the agent must achieve.

task_fields.name: acceptance_criteria
task_fields.description: List of MUST / NEVER statements that define when an output is considered valid.

task_fields.name: output_contract
task_fields.description: Description of allowed output format (e.g. plain text, bullets) and explicit exclusions.

task_fields.name: rule_block_references
task_fields.description: Optional list of referenced rule blocks by stable name (no rule text).

tier_patterns.name: tier2_task_plus_minimal_plan_pattern
tier_patterns.description: Task plus light Execution Plan for agents needing some structure and validation but no heavy rule blocks.

task_fields.name: objective
task_fields.description: Clear description of what the agent must deliver for the user.

task_fields.name: acceptance_criteria
task_fields.description: MUST / NEVER constraints, including high-level validation expectations (e.g. MUST cite FAQ source).

task_fields.name: output_contract
task_fields.description: Format and scope contract (e.g. text answers with inline citations, no JSON).

task_fields.name: rule_block_references
task_fields.description: Optional references to one or two light rule blocks (e.g. citation format, tone rules).

tier_patterns.name: tier3_task_plan_rules_pattern
tier_patterns.description: Full orthogonal pattern with Task, Execution Plan, and Rule Blocks for deterministic, schema-bound agents.

task_fields.name: objective
task_fields.description: Single-sentence statement of the deterministic outcome (e.g. generate precise quote, produce valid JSON).

task_fields.name: acceptance_criteria
task_fields.description: Explicit MUST / NEVER constraints including schema compliance, guardrails, and rule-application requirements.

task_fields.name: output_contract
task_fields.description: Strict format contract (e.g. JSON only, HTML only, Python code block only) and scope of allowed content.

task_fields.name: rule_block_references
task_fields.description: List of required rule block names that govern syntax, schemas, mappings, guardrails, markup, and tone.

tier_patterns.name: tier3_brief_writing_agent_pattern
tier_patterns.description: Specialized Tier 3 pattern for brand-specific brief-writing with citations, guardrails, and markup.

task_fields.name: objective
task_fields.description: Generate a comprehensive, compliant creative brief for a specific account with correct citations and gap highlighting.

task_fields.name: acceptance_criteria
task_fields.description: MUST include citations for all Supporting Data references; MUST mark missing information with gap highlights; MUST follow context source mapping; MUST apply brand guardrails; MUST output valid HTML using the brief structure schema; MUST NEVER fabricate unknowns (use GAP/TBD).

task_fields.name: output_contract
task_fields.description: HTML-only output with a fixed brief structure (BRIEF / SCOPE sections and subsections) and allowed tags restricted to headings, paragraphs, lists, mark, sup, br, hr.

task_fields.name: rule_block_references
task_fields.description: References to citation_formatting_rules, context_source_mapping_rules, highlight_formatting_rules, brand_guardrail_rules, markup_system_rules, and brief_html_structure_rules.

rule_blocks.name: brand_guardrail_rules
rule_blocks.description: Defines hard brand/account scope boundaries for an agent: how to determine whether a request is in-scope for {{company_name}}, what rejection message to use when it is not, and optional topic scoping for allowed request types. This block is applied as the first step in the Execution Plan and ensures no downstream processing occurs if the guardrail fails.

rule_blocks.name: context_source_mapping_rules
rule_blocks.description: Maps visible Supporting Data section headers to stable citation category labels (e.g. Creative Standards, Workflow Preferences, Recurrent Projects), defining how input markdown headings are normalized into a small, consistent set of provenance categories used in citations and structural decisions.

rule_blocks.name: citation_formatting_rules
rule_blocks.description: Specifies the HTML markup that must be used to represent citations: a <mark> with data-type="citation" (or equivalent) wrapping the cited text plus one or more <sup> elements carrying data-citation-id, data-id, data-type, data-title, and data-reasoning metadata. Includes rules for lists, hyperlinks, zero-nesting, and flattening of inner marks.

rule_blocks.name: citation_numbering_rules
rule_blocks.description: Defines how citation IDs and visual numbers are assigned and validated: sequential document-wide numbering (1, 2, 3, 4... ), unique data-citation-id linkage between mark and superscripts, prohibition on renumber gaps or reuse, and constraints around the Citations section listing categories in numerical order.

sections: Citation Formatting Rules – Global Sequential Numbering
Execution Plan Owns – Numbering and ID Integrity

rule_blocks.name: citation_metadata_rules
rule_blocks.description: Specifies the semantics and constraints of citation metadata attributes such as data-id, data-type, data-title, and data-reasoning: how they map to Supporting Data contextItem IDs, allowed types (brand_brain vs initial_prompt), reasoning length and content requirements, and restrictions on punctuation and formatting inside attribute values.

sections: Citation Formatting Rules – Metadata Attribute Definitions
Common Mistakes – Missing metadata on superscript

rule_blocks.name: citations_section_layout_rules
rule_blocks.description: Defines the optional trailing Citations section structure for agents that use a footer: its HTML skeleton (hr, heading, list of numbered category lines), ordering requirements, and the rule that only actually-used categories appear. For the current brief agent, this is mostly historical as inline-only provenance is preferred.

rule_blocks.name: highlight_formatting_rules
rule_blocks.description: Defines how gaps, unverified information, and uncertain content are marked using <mark class="highlight"> with appropriate data-type values (gap, citation, or default). It bans inline styles and Markdown ==...== syntax and provides canonical examples for GAP, TBD, and generic 'need to confirm' highlights.

rule_blocks.name: markup_system_rules
rule_blocks.description: General semantic HTML markup rules for mark-based annotations: allowed tags, required class and data-type attributes, constraints on overlapping types, and the separation of semantic markup from visual styling (delegating all CSS to the frontend). Encodes the one-type-per-mark rule and disallows inline styles or Markdown highlighting syntax.

rule_blocks.name: brief_attachments_rules
rule_blocks.description: Defines how attached documents, PDFs, and other files referenced in the initial customer brief should be handled: when they must be opened, how their content participates in context, and the expectation that tools are used to read them before generating the final brief.

rule_blocks.name: brief_html_structure_rules
rule_blocks.description: Defines the canonical internal brief structure (BRIEF vs SCOPE sections and their subsections) and how these map to HTML headings, paragraphs, and lists: required sections such as Problem Overview, Brand/Customer & Project Context, Target Audience, Creative Guidance, Deliverables, Volume, Timeline, Technical Specifications, Out of Scope, and Account resource files.

rule_blocks.name: brief_allowed_html_tags_rules
rule_blocks.description: Restricts the set of HTML tags the brief-writing agent may emit (h1–h3, p, ul, li, mark, sup, br, hr) and clarifies that no other tags, inline styles, or Markdown should be present. This allows the frontend to rely on a constrained, predictable HTML subset.

sections: Task Overview – Ensure structural and content integrity
Core Principle – Markup is semantic, styling is frontend

rule_blocks.name: archetype_governance_rules
rule_blocks.description: Captures non-procedural constraints for archetype and scope control in brief generation: how referenced projects anchor archetypes, what counts as baseline deliverables and objectives that cannot be removed, when additional deliverables may be introduced and must be highlighted, and how volume expansion and objective reframing are constrained.

rule_blocks.name: json_schema_rules
rule_blocks.description: Defines JSON schema structures for agents that output JSON (e.g. quoting agents): required fields, allowed data types, nesting, and object/array layouts. The Execution Plan refers to these rules for schema validation, while the Task references them as the contract for valid output.

rule_blocks.name: pricing_rules
rule_blocks.description: Holds the pricing and discount logic for quoting agents: how SKUs map to prices, rounding and currency conventions, which discounts are forbidden, and rules for never inferring unknown prices. The Task requires adherence to these rules; the Plan applies them.

sections: Quoting Agent – Rule Blocks – Pricing Rules
Rule Blocks Define How – Pricing Rules

rule_blocks.name: quantity_and_unit_rules
rule_blocks.description: Specifies valid quantity and unit combinations (e.g. hours, seats, units) and their validation constraints for quoting agents, ensuring that quantities are well-formed, non-negative, and consistent with each SKU or service type.

rule_blocks.name: python_coding_rules
rule_blocks.description: Defines coding conventions for Python code agents: modular function-only design, imports at top, main entrypoint structure, no hard-coded paths, error-handling patterns, docstring requirements, and naming conventions such as load_*, transform_*, write_*.

sections: Python Code Agent – Rule Blocks – Python Coding Rules

rule_blocks.name: sales_tone_rules
rule_blocks.description: Defines the tone and style for sales agents: friendly, consultative, never pushy, with constraints on hedging and prohibition on fabricated specs or prices. Task refers to this block by name; the Plan applies it when generating responses.

sections: Sales Agent – Rule Blocks – Sales Tone Rules
Quick Reference Table – Tone belongs in Rule Blocks

evaluation_rubrics.name: Brief Agent Structural & Rule Compliance
evaluation_rubrics.description: Evaluates whether a brief-writing agent’s output complies with Task acceptance criteria, Execution Plan validations, and Rule Blocks for structure, citations, markup, and guardrails.

dimensions.label: Brief Structure Compliance
dimensions.description: Does the output follow the required BRIEF/SCOPE section structure and include all mandatory subsections with correct hierarchy and ordering?
signals_positive: All required sections (Problem Overview, Brand/Customer & Project Context, Target Audience, Creative Guidance, Deliverables, Volume, Timeline, Technical Specifications, Out of Scope, Account resource files) are present and properly labeled.
Section hierarchy matches the defined brief_html_structure_rules (no reordering or merging of sections).
signals_negative: Missing one or more required sections or subsections.
Sections merged or renamed in ways that break mapping to the structure schema.
Out-of-contract content (e.g. extra top-level sections) without clear justification.

anchor_examples.1: Major structural violations; brief cannot be reliably parsed.
anchor_examples.3: Minor structural deviations but overall shape recognizable.
anchor_examples.5: Fully compliant with structure schema; no deviations.

dimensions.label: Brand Guardrail Compliance
dimensions.description: Did the agent correctly enforce brand/account guardrails, rejecting out-of-scope requests and not mixing brands or topics?
signals_positive: For in-scope requests, brief content refers only to the target {{company_name}}.
For out-of-scope requests, agent returns the standard rejection message and does not process further.
No references to other brands or accounts when guardrail forbids it.
signals_negative: Agent generates a brief for a different brand than specified in guardrails.
Agent partially processes an out-of-scope request before rejecting.
Soft rejections that offer generic help in violation of hard-guardrail rules.

dimensions.label: Citation Formatting & Provenance
dimensions.description: Checks adherence to citation_formatting_rules, context_source_mapping_rules, citation_numbering_rules, and citation_metadata_rules.
signals_positive: Every factual statement derived from Supporting Data or the initial prompt has at least one properly-formed mark + sup pair.
Citations are sequentially numbered, with consistent data-citation-id links between marks and superscripts.
data-id/data-type/data-title/data-reasoning attributes are present and correctly reflect their sources.
Categories in any footer or summary section match the mapping defined in context_source_mapping_rules.
signals_negative: Missing citations for factual statements clearly derived from Supporting Data.
Non-sequential or duplicated citation numbering.
Missing or malformed metadata attributes (e.g. no data-reasoning, wrong data-type).
Incorrect mapping of Supporting Data sections to categories (e.g. mapping a tone section to Recurrent Projects).

dimensions.label: Highlight & Markup Compliance
dimensions.description: Assesses whether gaps and uncertainties are correctly marked and markup_system_rules / highlight_formatting_rules are respected.
signals_positive: All missing or ambiguous fields are marked with <mark class="highlight" data-type="gap">…</mark> or equivalent.
No inline styles or Markdown ==...== syntax are used; only semantic HTML mark tags.
Citation and gap marks do not overlap or reuse conflicting data-type values.
signals_negative: Known gaps in the input are not highlighted.
Multiple data-type values on a single mark or nested marks of different types.
Use of spans, inline styles, or markdown highlighting instead of mark tags.

dimensions.label: Schema & Allowed Tags Compliance
dimensions.description: Evaluates whether only the allowed HTML tags and structures defined in brief_allowed_html_tags_rules are used and the output is syntactically valid.
signals_positive: Only allowed tags (h1–h3, p, ul, li, mark, sup, br, hr) appear in the output.
HTML parses cleanly and nesting is well-formed.
No stray Markdown, scripts, or unsupported elements.
signals_negative: Presence of unsupported tags or raw Markdown.
Malformed HTML that breaks parsing.
Embedded styling or script content.

comments.description: Freeform evaluator notes about major failures or strengths.

evaluation_rubrics.name: Brief Agent Content Quality
evaluation_rubrics.description: Evaluates semantic quality of the generated brief: accuracy, relevance, tone alignment, and usefulness, independent of strict structural compliance.

dimensions.label: Factual Accuracy vs Inputs
dimensions.description: Degree to which statements match the initial customer brief, attachments, and Supporting Data without hallucination.
signals_positive: Key facts (brand, objectives, deliverables, constraints) match the input sources.
No new factual claims are introduced without support.
Any recommendations beyond baseline are clearly marked and conservative.
signals_negative: Incorrect or fabricated deadlines, volumes, or deliverables.
Attribution of requirements to Supporting Data when they do not exist in the sources.
Contradictions between brief content and cited sources.

dimensions.label: Relevance & Focus
dimensions.description: Whether the brief stays on-topic and concentrates on the project archetype and scope implied by the initial request and referenced projects.
signals_positive: Content focuses on the correct project type and channels.
No digressions into unrelated campaigns or brands.
Scope aligns with archetype_governance_rules (no silent expansions or removals).
signals_negative: Inclusion of clearly unrelated marketing ideas or off-topic recommendations.
Changing the fundamental objective or project type without justification.
Adding large new deliverable families without highlighting or clear basis.

dimensions.label: Tone & Style Alignment
dimensions.description: Alignment with the Customer Tone of Voice & Writing Style specified in Supporting Data and implied by the user’s initial brief.
signals_positive: Language and phrasing match the customer’s tone (e.g. formal vs casual).
Sections read like internal briefs used by the design team.
No excessive hedging or all-caps; respects capitalization and hedging rules where defined.
signals_negative: Tone that clashes with the brand or previous briefs.
Overuse of all caps, exclamation marks, or slang where not appropriate.
Dry or robotic style when a more human-internal style is expected.

dimensions.label: Actionability for Design Team
dimensions.description: How well the brief enables a designer to start work without major clarification: clarity of objectives, audience, deliverables, and constraints.
signals_positive: Objectives, audience, deliverables, and technical specs are concrete and unambiguous where information exists.
Gaps are clearly highlighted so designers know what to ask for.
Brief avoids vague, high-level statements when input provides specifics.
signals_negative: Key fields are left vague even when source data is concrete.
Designers would need to re-read the original customer request to understand the task.
Important constraints (formats, channels, deadlines) are missing or unclear without being marked as gaps.

evaluation_rubrics.name: Orthogonality & Tier Compliance
evaluation_rubrics.description: Evaluator rubric for checking whether a given system prompt respects the orthogonality pattern and matches its declared tier.

dimensions.label: Task Component Purity
dimensions.description: Does the Task block contain only WHAT (objective, acceptance criteria, output contract, rule-block references) and no HOW or rule syntax?
signals_positive: Task contains clear objective, MUST/NEVER statements, and output contract.
No procedural steps, branching logic, or regex/syntax appear in Task.
Rule blocks are referenced only by stable names.
signals_negative: Task includes numbered steps, if/else logic, or detailed parsing instructions.
Rule syntax (regex, JSON schema) embedded directly in Task.
Examples or large output samples inside Task.

dimensions.label: Execution Plan Component Purity
dimensions.description: Does the Execution Plan contain only HOW (steps, validation flows, branching) without embedding rule text, schemas, or tone instructions?
signals_positive: Plan is expressed as ordered atomic steps with clear action_names.
Validation responsibilities (preflight, schema checks, numbering) are present where needed.
Plan refers to rule blocks by name for schemas, markup, and pricing.
signals_negative: Plan repeats rule content or schemas that already live in rule blocks.
Plan contains examples or large sample outputs.
Tone, style, or formatting rules are written directly in the Plan.

dimensions.label: Rule Block Component Purity
dimensions.description: Do Rule Blocks restrict themselves to syntax, schemas, mappings, and minimal examples, without procedural validation steps or acceptance criteria?
signals_positive: Rule blocks define schemas, markup rules, mappings, numbering, or regex constraints.
Only minimal examples are present, used purely to illustrate syntax.
No if/else logic, step ordering, or guardrail procedures appear in rule blocks.
signals_negative: Rule blocks describe sequences of actions or validation procedures.
Task-like acceptance criteria (MUST/NEVER) embedded in rule blocks.
Large, full-length examples that really belong in docs or training data.

dimensions.label: Tier Match to Requirements
dimensions.description: Whether the chosen tier (1/2/3) matches the actual complexity and determinism requirements of the agent.
signals_positive: Tier 1 used only for low-risk, creative agents without strict schemas.
Tier 2 used for lightly structured agents with limited validation needs.
Tier 3 used when deterministic formats, schemas, and multi-step validation are required.
signals_negative: Tier 1 used despite needing JSON or HTML schemas and validation.
Tier 2 used while relying heavily on rule blocks without separation.
Tier 3 over-specified for simple, exploratory agents with no schema.

tier_patterns_markdown_summary: Tier 1 templates define only Task (objective, acceptance criteria, output contract) and are suited for creative, low-risk agents. Tier 2 adds a minimal Execution Plan for light structure and validation but keeps rule blocks to a minimum. Tier 3 fully separates Task, Plan, and Rule Blocks and is used when deterministic formats, schemas, guardrails, and multi-step validation are required; the brief-writing agent is a canonical Tier 3 example.
